text: The surgeon clamps the splenic artery at the initial stage of a spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy, visibly reducing blood inflow to the splenic vessels to minimize bleeding during subsequent dissection.
The surgeon identifies the splenic artery using fluorescence imaging and clamps it with a bulldog clamp, reducing blood inflow to the splenic vessels.

The surgeon begins the dissection to separate the spleen vessels from the pancreas, carefully navigating strong adhesions between these structures. The dissection proceeds close to the spleen, revealing multiple branches of spleen veins.

The surgeon controls bleeding by using the splenic artery first approach, which involves clamping the splenic artery with a bulldog clamp to decrease blood inflow. Once bleeding is controlled, the clamp is removed.
